segments.s077-g49: "که" here is the subordinating particle 'that / as' introducing the clause; "گفتی" is the 2nd-person past tense 'you said' (classical usage 'که گفتی' = 'as you said') — the provided lemma senses did not include this verbal sense. "همی" is the archaic/prosodic adverb/prefix marking continued or emphatic action (classical imperfective/iterative sense) and links to the following verb. "جان" is the direct object 'life(s)'. "برافشاندند" is 3rd-person plural past ('they scattered/threw forth') formed from افشاندن with the prefix بر-; together "جان برافشاندند" is a poetic idiom meaning to pour out or expend their lives (to be overcome, to offer themselves).
segments.s077-g50: "ز هر جای" is a prepositional phrase 'from every place' (ز = poetic از). "رامشگران" is a plural agent noun 'those who bring joy/comfort' (minstrels/entertainers in this celebratory context) and functions as the (implicit) subject here. "خواندند" is 3rd-person plural past 'they sang / performed'; because there is no object marker را, the verb is best read intransitively with "رامشگران" as its subject ('the minstrels sang from every place').
segments.s077-g1: همی + راند: همی is the archaic/classical prefix that gives a continuous or emphatic sense to the verb (literally 'kept / was'), so همی راند means 'he kept driving/urging on' rather than a single momentary action. دستان گرفته is a participial phrase 'having taken the hands' (i.e. 'gripping the reins' or 'clutching with his hands'); گرفته is the perfect participle modifying دستان. شتاب (a noun) here is adverbialized ('with haste' / 'hastening') and attaches to the verb phrase to describe manner.
segments.s077-g2: چو introduces a simile ('like' / 'as'). The line gives two parallel similes joined by و: first چو + پرنده مرغ ('like a bird'), then چو + کشتی برآب ('like a ship on the water'). برآب is a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'on/over the water.' Note the poetic redundancy (پرنده مرغ) for emphasis.
segments.s077-g3: کسی را places 'no one' in the role of experiencer/possessor (را here is the object/definiteness marker). نبد is an archaic negative copula meaning 'was not' or 'there was not,' so کسی را نبد ز آمدنش آگهی = 'no one had information about his arrival.' ز + آمدنش is the genitive/ablative 'of his coming' (آمدن + enclitic ش = 'his coming').
segments.s077-g4: پذیره (reception) is the noun; نرفتند is the past 3rd‑plural with negative prefix ن‑ ('they did not go'), so here it means 'they did not go (to receive)'. با + فرهی ('with nobility / with cultured/noble behavior') indicates the manner of reception that did not occur. In short: 'they did not go to welcome him with noble courtesy.'
segments.s077-g5: خروشی (a cry/clamor) is the subject and برآمد is the past verb 'arose/issued forth.' ز introduces the source 'from' and پرده سرای = 'the curtain of the chamber/room.' Word order is typical of classical Persian: subject + verb + source phrase. (Note: the provided senses list for سرای does not include 'house/chamber'; that specific sense is not given, so it cannot be matched.)
segments.s077-g6: که introduces an explanatory/relative clause ('for / that / namely'), آمد is simple past 'came,' ز ره = 'by way of / from the road,' and زال is the proper name. فرخنده‑رای is an epithet placed after the name meaning 'auspicious / fair‑faced' and qualifies زال. The clause gives the reason for the commotion: Zal had arrived by the road.
segments.s077-g7: پذیره شدش is a light passive/impersonal construction: پذیره (reception) + شد (became/occurred) + ـش (enclitic pronoun 'him') yields 'he was received' or 'a reception befell him.' Following that, the words سام یل شادمان are the nominative subject phrase 'Sam, the hero, joyful' — i.e. Sam the brave, rejoicing. So the line means that Sam received him gladly. The enclitic ـش attached to شد refers to the person received.
segments.s077-g8: همی داشت is the iterative/continuous past ('he kept / was holding'); اندر introduces a locative 'inside/within.' برش here functions to mark the person kept (object pronoun sense 'him' or 'for him') in this phrase, and یک زمان = 'for a time / a moment.' So the whole segment means 'he kept him inside for a time.' (Because the dataset's senses for برش do not include the pronoun/object sense, that particular word's sense cannot be matched to the given options.)
segments.s077-g25: This is a simple past clause: فرستاده‌ای آمد literally 'a messenger came.' از نزد اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from the presence/of him' where نزد takes 'او' (here written اوی) to mean 'in the presence of / from the court of.' Word order is subject (فرستاده‌ای) + past verb (آمد) + origin phrase (از نزد اوی).
segments.s077-g26: که introduces reported content ('that / saying that'). پردخته شد is a compound verbal idea: پردخته (perfect/composed, i.e. 'settled, arranged') + شد (past 'became/was'), so together 'was settled/finished.' کار is the noun 'affair/ business' and بنمای روی is an injunction: بنمای (imperative or jussive 'show/reveal') + روی ('face' = 'appear in person'). The clause as a whole reports the messenger's message: 'that the affair was settled — show your face.'
segments.s077-g27: کنون 'now' starts the question. چیست is copular 'what is' (چی + ست), followed by پاسخ 'answer' and فرستاده را 'to the messenger' where را marks the definite object/target of the question: 'What now is the answer to the messenger?' The word order is adverb (کنون) + interrogative copula (چیست) + object phrase.
segments.s077-g28: چه 'what' begins a second question clause. گوییم here is the verb 'we say / shall say' in present/subjunctive use (the lemma list given is nominal, so the verbal sense is not listed). مهراب آزاده را is the addressee 'to Mehrab Azadeh' with را marking the object/recipient. The whole clause: 'What shall we say to Mehrab Azadeh?'
segments.s077-g29: ز شادی 'from/with joy' gives the cause. چنان شد 'became thus' is a standard past construction indicating a resulting state; دل زال سام is the compound subject 'the heart of Zal and Sam' (two names). Word order is causal phrase + result verb + subject (poetic flexibility).
segments.s077-g30: که here introduces the descriptive/result clause 'so that...' رنگش 'his color/complexion' + سراپای 'all over / head to foot' + شد 'became' + لعل فام 'ruby‑hued' (a compound adjective). The clause describes the outward effect: 'so that his whole complexion became ruby-colored.'
segments.s077-g31: چنین داد پاسخ is 'thus he gave answer' (چنین 'thus' + داد 'gave' + پاسخ 'answer'). The following که‌ای پهلوان is the vocative/introduction to direct speech: که (introducing the speech) + ای (vocative particle attached) + پهلوان 'O champion.' This is a formulaic way to report direct speech.
segments.s077-g32: گر is the conditional 'if.' ایدون is an archaic/poetic verbal form meaning 'you come / you should come / you come here' (a subjunctive/imperative sense). که here links the conditional to the result clause. بینی is 2nd‑person present/subjunctive 'you see' (not 'nose'), and به روشن‌روان is a prepositional phrase 'with/for a clear‑soul / toward the clear‑souled (one),' an epithet; together the clause reads 'If you come and see with a clear soul...' Note the poetic, archaic verb forms.
segments.s077-g9: فرود آمد is a past‑tense compound: فرود (down/landing) + آمد (came) = 'he descended/ alighted'. از باره is a short prepositional phrase ('from/on that occasion' — poetic use of از + باره). بوسید is a simple past verb 'he kissed' and خاک is its direct object 'the earth/ground', following the verb (typical Persian V…O order in narrative). The whole segment narrates two consecutive actions ('he came down' then 'kissed the earth').
segments.s077-g10: بگفت is an archaic past form meaning 'he said' (ب‑ prefix appears in classical Persian narratives). آن is the demonstrative 'that/the'; کجا is the interrogative 'where'. دید و بشنید are coordinated past verbs 'saw and heard' (third person singular) joined by و. پاک is an adjective ('pure' here) modifying the implied thing being seen/heard. The segment reports his spoken question/remark (introduced by the previous actions).
segments.s077-g11: نشست is a past tense verb 'he sat' (or 'took a seat'). The prepositional phrase از بر تخت پرمایه precedes the person and means 'upon the richly‑furnished throne' (پرمایه = 'substantial/richly appointed'). Classical prose/poetry often places the verb before the subject, so the name سام (the subject) comes after this phrase.
segments.s077-g12: ابا here functions as an appositive/title ('father of') attached to زال, identifying سام as Zal's father (this reading is idiomatic/classical and not covered by the provided senses, so treated as irregular). خرم دل is a two‑word adjectival phrase 'joyful‑hearted' where خرم modifies دل; و links the next adjective شادکام 'content/fortunate'. Together they characterize Sam after the verb 'sat'.
segments.s077-g13: سخنهای سیندخت is a genitive phrase 'the words/utterances of Sindukht' (سخنهای = plural/construct of سخن). گفتن گرفت uses گرفت as a light verb meaning 'began to' (classical narrative idiom): گفتن گرفت = 'began to speak'. The segment says that the speaking of Sindukht's words commenced.
segments.s077-g14: لبش 'her lip(s)/mouth' is the subject of the next clause; گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'became/turned' (change of state). خندان is the predicate adjective 'smiling'. نهفتن گرفت repeats the light‑verb pattern (نهفتن = 'to hide/withhold') + گرفت = 'began to hide/withhold'. The two coordinated clauses describe an outward smile and then an act of withholding.
segments.s077-g15: چنین گفت is a formulaic opening 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت used narratively as 'said'). کامد is an archaic/past form meaning 'came/arrived' (literary), followed by ز کابل 'from Kabul' and پیام 'a message/tidings' as the subject/thing that arrived. The clause introduces the content of his speech: a message arrived from Kabul.
segments.s077-g16: پیمبر here means 'messenger' (classical use) rather than 'prophet'. زنی is 'a woman' (identifying the messenger). بود is the past copula 'was'. سیندخت نام is a nominative apposition 'named Sindukht' (نام = 'name'); together the clause states that the messenger was a woman called Sindukht.
segments.s077-g17: ز من خواست: 'ز' is a poetic preposition 'from', so the phrase reads 'she asked from me'; 'پیمان' (object) is placed before the verb 'خواست' (past 3rd) — a common poetic/object‑preposing order in Persian. 'و دادم زمان' follows: 'دادم' is first‑person past 'I gave', and 'زمان' (literally 'time') here is used poetically with 'دادم' to mean 'I gave my word/pledge' (an idiomatic collocation in older Persian).
segments.s077-g18: This is a subordinate clause introduced by 'که' ('that'). 'هرگز' = 'never'. 'نباشم' is the negative present/subjunctive form of 'بودن' used here as a pledge/intent ('that I should not be / I will not be'); 'بدو' is the dative 'to her' and 'بدگمان' is the adjective 'suspicious' that follows the dative. Word order places the verb before the dative+adjective (typical of Persian subordinate clauses).
segments.s077-g19: 'ز هر چیز' = 'of/from every thing'; 'کز' is the relative 'that/which' introducing the clause 'that asked of me'. 'به خوبی' is a phrase of manner/benefit ('for/to/with kindness' or 'for goodness'), and 'بخواست' is past third‑person 'asked/desired'. The relative clause modifies 'هر چیز'.
segments.s077-g20: 'سخنها' (plural 'words/sayings') is the topic/object; 'بران' ('upon/thereon') links to the preceding clause ('about those things'); 'برنهادیم' is first‑person plural past 'we raised/put forth/uttered'. 'راست' qualifies the utterance ('true' / 'truly'). Note the poetic order: topic + directional 'بران' + verb at the end.
segments.s077-g21: 'نخست' opens an enumerative clause ('first'). 'آنکه' functions as 'that which / that he who' introducing the content. 'با ماه کابلستان' is a prepositional phrase 'with the moon of Kabulistan' (ماه used poetically for a beautiful person). 'کابلستان' is the place/name in genitive relation.
segments.s077-g22: 'شود' is the third‑person singular present/subjunctive or future marker 'become / will be'. It is followed by the predicate nominal 'جفت خورشید زابلستان' = 'the pair/match of the sun of Zabulistan' (poetic omission of the ezafe is common). Word order here places the verb before the noun phrase that serves as predicate.
segments.s077-g23: 'دگر آنکه' = 'another [thing] is that...' introducing a second item. 'زی' here is the older/prepositional 'from/of/because of' (classical Persian), so 'زی او' means 'because of her' or 'on her account'. 'به مهمان شویم' uses 'شویم' (first‑person plural subjunctive/present) 'we become' with 'به مهمان' ('into guests' = 'become guests').
segments.s077-g24: 'بران' ('thereupon' / 'on that account') links result; 'دردها' is the plural object 'the pains/troubles'. 'پاک' functions adverbially 'completely/entirely' and 'درمان شویم' is a passive/state expression 'we be cured / become healed'. Together: 'thereupon the pains will be wholly cured (for us)'.
segments.s077-g41: Poetic inverted word order: the object هیونی (beasts/animals) appears first, then the verb برافگند, and finally the subject مرد دلیر. برافگند is an archaic past tense (equivalent to modern بر افکند/افکند) meaning “scattered/threw off.” مرد دلیر is a topicalized subject phrase meaning “the brave/valiant man.”
segments.s077-g42: بدان functions here as a compact adverbial/introductory particle (“therewith/thereby” or “with that”) leading into a purpose/result clause introduced by تا. تا شود is a purposive/result clause: تا + شود (شود = become/happen) = “so that he became.” نزد is a preposition meaning “before/in the presence of / near,” مهراب is used as a proper name (here the person Mehrab), and شیر is a simile/epithet “lion.”
segments.s077-g43: بگوید که is a formulaic reportive clause (classical narration: “it is said/they say that”). آمد is the simple past “came/arrived.” سپهبد is the title ‘spahbed’ (general) and ز راه is a poetic prepositional phrase “from/by the road.” The clause uses straightforward past narration.
segments.s077-g44: ابا زال here functions as a proper name (Aba Zal); the sequence is name + epithet. با is the preposition “with,” پیل is “elephant,” و is the coordinating conjunction “and,” while چندی سپاه = “some troops/army” (چندی = several/some, سپاه = host/army). The phrase states accompaniment (“with an elephant and some troops”).
segments.s077-g45: فرستاده تازان is a compact genitive/attributive phrase (the messenger of/for the تازان — ‘raiders’ or ‘mounted warriors’); poetry often omits the ezafe. به کابل رسید is a simple past motion clause: به + place (to Kabul) + رسید (arrived/reached).
segments.s077-g46: خروشی برآمد is a verbal clause: خروشی (a roar/commotion) + برآمد (arose/issued forth). چنان چون is a paired adverbial (چنان = thus/so, چون = as/like) intensifying the comparison, and سزید is a past form meaning “it befitted/it deserved” (i.e. “as was fitting/appropriate”). The line gives a customary evaluative tag: a commotion arose, as was appropriate.
segments.s077-g47: چنان شاد شد is a simple result clause: چنان = thus/so, شاد = glad/joyful, شد = became. شاه کابلستان is a nominal phrase ‘the king of Kabulistan’ (title + region). Word order is typical poetic: adverb + predicate + subject.
segments.s077-g48: ز پیوند خورشید زابلستان explains cause/occasion: ز (here = because of/from), پیوند = bond/union (often marriage or alliance in epic contexts), خورشید is used as a proper name (Khoršīd) though literally ‘sun,’ and زابلستان is the toponym ‘Zabulistan.’ Together: “because of the bond/alliance with Khoršīd of Zabulistan.”
segments.s077-g33: This clause coordinates two subjects (سپه رانی ‘the army’ + ما ‘we’) before the verb شویم placed at the end — a common Classical/poetic word order. شویم is 1st‑person plural present/subjunctive (here jussive/hortative) meaning roughly “let us go / we shall go.” به کابل is a goal phrase ('towards/into Kabul') introduced before the final verb.
segments.s077-g34: بگوییم is 1st‑person plural subjunctive/hortative of گفتن used here as a following action 'we will say/announce' or 'let us say.' The following phrase زین در سخن بشنویم is coordinated with it: زین در (lit. 'in/at this place') modifies سخن ('speech, discourse'), and بشنویم is another 1st‑person plural subjunctive meaning 'we shall hear' or 'let us hear.' The two verbs create a sequence of intended actions.
segments.s077-g35: This is a past action: نگه کرد is a light‑verb construction where نگه (a noun 'holding/keeping') combines with کرد to mean 'he held' or 'kept in his hands.' The preposition به + دستان ('into/onto his hands') marks the place/means. فرخنده is an epithet 'fortunate/auspicious' placed before the proper name سام in apposition.
segments.s077-g36: بدانست is simple past 'he understood/knew.' What follows is an indirect interrogative/free relative clause giving its content: 'کورا ازین چیست کام' = 'what Kura's desire is about this.' The copula ست (is) follows the predicate کام ('desire'), a common Classical/Persian order where the verb/copy appears after the predicate.
segments.s077-g37: سخن is the main noun 'speech/tale.' هر چه از دخت مهراب is a free relative ('whatever concerning Mehrab's daughter'), and the clause ends with نیست, negating the whole: 'whatever is said about Mehrab's daughter does not exist/is not true.' Word order places the head noun before its qualifying clause.
segments.s077-g38: به نزدیک زال means 'in/at Zal's presence' or 'near Zal' (به + نزدیک marking proximity). آن refers back to the foregoing report ('that [story]'), and جز از خواب نیست is an exclusive negation: 'it is nothing except from sleep' = 'it is nothing but a dream.' The final نیست negates reality, restricting it to dream‑status.
segments.s077-g39: بفرمود is an honorific/simple past 'he commanded/ordered.' تا introduces the subordinate clause of command/purpose ('that ...'). زنگ و هندی are two coordinated agent nouns (here performers/slaves/attendants); درای is an adjective 'possessing/wealthy' modifying them (in classical usage adjectives can follow or precede; here it follows).
segments.s077-g40: Two past verbs are coordinated: زدند ('they struck' / 'they sounded' — often used for beating drums or giving signal) and گشادند ('they opened' — here a transitive result on پرده). پرده سرای is a noun phrase 'the curtain of the hall' (pرده modified by سرای); the verbs together describe the ordered actions (sound the signal and open the hall's curtain).

sentences.s077-s25: As you said, they poured out their lives; from every place the minstrels sang.
sentences.s077-s1: He urged on, clutching the reins and hastening — like a bird, like a ship upon the water.
sentences.s077-s2: No one had news of his coming; they did not go to receive him with noble courtesy.
sentences.s077-s3: A commotion arose from the chamber's curtain, for Zal, auspicious of mien, had come along the road.
sentences.s077-s4: Sam the hero received him joyfully, and for a time kept him inside.
sentences.s077-s13: A messenger came from his presence, saying that the affair had been settled — show your face.
sentences.s077-s14: Now what is the reply to the messenger? What shall we say to Mehrab Azadeh?
sentences.s077-s15: From joy Zal and Sam's hearts became so that his whole complexion turned ruby‑hued.
sentences.s077-s16: Thus he gave answer: 'O champion, if you come (here) and see with a clear soul...'
sentences.s077-s5: He descended, kissed the earth, and said, "Where could one have seen and heard anything so pure?"
sentences.s077-s6: Sam, father of Zal, sat on the richly appointed throne, joyful of heart and content.
sentences.s077-s7: She began to speak Sindukht's words; her lips turned smiling, and she began to withhold (them).
sentences.s077-s8: Thus he said: a message came from Kabul — the messenger was a woman named Sindukht.
sentences.s077-s9: She asked a pledge from me, and I gave my word that I would never be suspicious of her.
sentences.s077-s10: Of every thing that asked kindness of me, we set forth truthful words about it.
sentences.s077-s11: First, that the moon of Kabulistan should become the mate of the sun of Zabulistan.
sentences.s077-s12: Another (promise): that because of her we should become guests, and thereby be completely cured of those pains.
sentences.s077-s21: The brave man scattered the beasts, so that he became a lion before Mehrab.
sentences.s077-s22: It is said that the spahbed came by the road; Aba Zal came with an elephant and some troops.
sentences.s077-s23: A messenger of the raiders arrived at Kabul; a roar arose, just as was fitting.
sentences.s077-s24: Thus the king of Kabulistan became joyful because of the alliance (bond) with Khoršīd of Zabulistan.
sentences.s077-s17: Let the army and we go to Kabul; we will speak there and hear words.
sentences.s077-s18: Sam the fortunate took her in his hands, and he knew what Kura's desire was in this matter.
sentences.s077-s19: Whatever is said about Mehrab's daughter is not true; in Zal's presence that is nothing but a dream.
sentences.s077-s20: He ordered that the Zang and the Indians (servants/performers) strike (their instruments) and draw open the curtain of the hall.
